verses.text: Again, David gathered together all the chosen men of Israel, thirty thousand. And David arose, and went with all the people that were with him from Baale of Judah, to bring up from thence the ark of God, whose name is called by the name of the LORD of hosts that dwelleth between the cherubims. Guidance: Thirty thousand picked men — a national honor guard. The ark of God has been at Kiriath-jearim (also called 'Baale of Judah,' a town about ten miles west of Jerusalem) for over seventy years, since the Philistines returned it in 1 Samuel 7:1-2. Saul never moved it; Israel's spiritual center remained outside the political capital throughout his entire reign. David's first major religious act as king of the unified kingdom is to bring the ark up to Jerusalem. The political-religious logic is precise: Jerusalem is the king's city, the geographic center of the new kingdom; the ark must join the city for Jerusalem to become Israel's spiritual capital as well as its political one. The narrator's title for the LORD here is striking: 'whose name is called by the name of the LORD of hosts that dwelleth between the cherubims.' The LORD is named by His ark-presence — the LORD whose throne is the mercy seat between the gold cherubim wings on top of the ark. The whole chapter is about bringing the throne of the LORD into the city of the king of Israel.

Again, David gathered together all the chosen men of Israel, thirty thousand. And David arose, and went with all the people that were with him from Baale of Judah, to bring up from thence the ark of God, whose name is called by the name of the LORD of hosts that dwelleth between the cherubims. Guidance: Thirty thousand picked men — a national honor guard. The ark of God has been at Kiriath-jearim (also called 'Baale of Judah,' a town about ten miles west of Jerusalem) for over seventy years, since the Philistines returned it in 1 Samuel 7:1-2. Saul never moved it; Israel's spiritual center remained outside the political capital throughout his entire reign. David's first major religious act as king of the unified kingdom is to bring the ark up to Jerusalem. The political-religious logic is precise: Jerusalem is the king's city, the geographic center of the new kingdom; the ark must join the city for Jerusalem to become Israel's spiritual capital as well as its political one. The narrator's title for the LORD here is striking: 'whose name is called by the name of the LORD of hosts that dwelleth between the cherubims.' The LORD is named by His ark-presence — the LORD whose throne is the mercy seat between the gold cherubim wings on top of the ark. The whole chapter is about bringing the throne of the LORD into the city of the king of Israel.

And they set the ark of God upon a new cart, and brought it out of the house of Abinadab that was in Gibeah: and Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, drave the new cart. And they brought it out of the house of Abinadab which was at Gibeah, accompanying the ark of God: and Ahio went before the ark. Guidance: The transport method is the chapter's first problem. 'They set the ark of God upon a new cart.' The Torah is explicit: the ark must be carried on poles by Levite Kohathites, never on a cart (Num 4:5-15, Num 7:9, Deut 10:8). The only ark-on-cart precedent is when the Philistines returned it (1 Sam 6:7) — a pagan transport method God permitted because pagans were ignorant. David, however, knows the Torah. He has chosen the Philistine method, presumably because the cart looks more dignified than poles and the distance is too far for porters. The 'new cart' detail is reverence; they got a vehicle never used for anything else. But reverence cannot substitute for obedience. Abinadab's house at Gibeah (or Gibeah-Kiriath-jearim, a height of the same town) had been the ark's home for seventy years. Uzzah and Ahio, his sons (likely his grandsons, given the time elapsed), drive the cart. Ahio walks in front. The procession begins. Everything looks dignified. The chapter is going to expose the gap between dignified-looking practice and obedient practice.

And they set the ark of God upon a new cart, and brought it out of the house of Abinadab that was in Gibeah: and Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, drave the new cart. And they brought it out of the house of Abinadab which was at Gibeah, accompanying the ark of God: and Ahio went before the ark. Guidance: The transport method is the chapter's first problem. 'They set the ark of God upon a new cart.' The Torah is explicit: the ark must be carried on poles by Levite Kohathites, never on a cart (Num 4:5-15, Num 7:9, Deut 10:8). The only ark-on-cart precedent is when the Philistines returned it (1 Sam 6:7) — a pagan transport method God permitted because pagans were ignorant. David, however, knows the Torah. He has chosen the Philistine method, presumably because the cart looks more dignified than poles and the distance is too far for porters. The 'new cart' detail is reverence; they got a vehicle never used for anything else. But reverence cannot substitute for obedience. Abinadab's house at Gibeah (or Gibeah-Kiriath-jearim, a height of the same town) had been the ark's home for seventy years. Uzzah and Ahio, his sons (likely his grandsons, given the time elapsed), drive the cart. Ahio walks in front. The procession begins. Everything looks dignified. The chapter is going to expose the gap between dignified-looking practice and obedient practice.

And David and all the house of Israel played before the LORD on all manner of instruments made of fir wood, even on harps, and on psalteries, and on timbrels, and on cornets, and on cymbals. Guidance: A national worship procession. 'David and all the house of Israel' — the whole community of the unified kingdom — are participating. Six instruments are named (harp, psaltery, timbrel, cornet, cymbal — the standard temple-orchestra inventory that will solidify in Solomon's reign). 'Fir wood' instruments — probably cypress, the standard wood for portable musical instruments in the period. The celebration is joyful, loud, public, and theologically correct in intent. The mistake is not in the joy; the mistake is in the transport. The chapter is showing us that worship can be enthusiastic, communal, and well-intentioned and still be wrong in a critical detail — and the LORD's response will be hard for David and for us to read. Worship offered with the wrong instructions, however joyful, is not the same as worship offered correctly. The chapter is about to teach this lesson the hardest way.

And when they came to Nachon's threshingfloor, Uzzah put forth his hand to the ark of God, and took hold of it; for the oxen shook it. And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Uzzah; and God smote him there for his error; and there he died by the ark of God. Guidance: At Nachon's threshing-floor (probably a leveled rock-platform where wheat was threshed), the oxen 'shook' the cart — perhaps stumbled on a stone or shied at something on the path. The ark threatens to slide off. Uzzah reflexively reaches out his hand to steady it. The reflex is human, instinctive, protective. 'The anger of the LORD was kindled against Uzzah; and God smote him there for his error; and there he died by the ark of God.' Uzzah falls dead at the side of the cart. The reader feels the shock the same way David is about to feel it. The act of reaching to steady the ark seemed reverent — better than letting the ark crash to the ground. But the Torah's instruction was that the ark was holy and untouchable; only the Kohathite priests carrying the ark by its poles could come near it; even they were forbidden to touch it (Num 4:15). Uzzah's hand violated the holiness boundary. The deeper issue: if the ark had been on poles as God commanded, the oxen never would have been near it, and Uzzah's reflex would never have been needed. Uzzah dies because of David's earlier shortcut. The chapter is showing us that improvised reverence does not produce safer worship; it produces predictable disasters whose victims are not always the ones who chose the shortcut. Uzzah probably knew better; he had grown up with the ark in his house. But the procession had momentum and the cart was already moving. Some of the deepest grief in chapter is going to be that Uzzah was, in some sense, a casualty of a king's well-meaning error rather than his own. The narrator does not blame Uzzah individually; he calls it 'his error' (shal — the Hebrew is hard to translate; it may mean 'rash act' or simply 'lapse'). But the verse remains uncomfortable. We are being shown that God's holiness is not a metaphor and the priestly transport rules are not antiquarian detail. The same God who delivered David at Baal-perazim and the Rephaim valley is also a God whose presence in the ark is so holy that an unauthorized hand on it produces immediate death. Worship that does not reckon with both sides of God's character — kindness and consuming holiness — is unprepared for what worship actually is.

And David was displeased, because the LORD had made a breach upon Uzzah: and he called the name of the place Perez-uzzah to this day. Guidance: 'David was displeased' — Hebrew vayyichar le-David, literally 'and it burned to David.' The same verb used for divine anger in v7 is now used for David's reaction. He is angry — at God, at himself, at the situation. He cannot yet read the event theologically; he reads it emotionally. He names the place 'Perez-uzzah' — 'the breach of Uzzah.' The same word for 'breach' (perez) David had used in joy at chapter 5:20 (Baal-perazim — 'the LORD has broken forth as a breach of waters'); now the breach has come the other direction — God breaking forth on Israel. The narrator's note 'unto this day' tells us the place-name survived for generations as a landmark of the disaster. David's displeasure is honest. He had organized a national worship procession in good faith; God killed a participant; David does not yet understand. The chapter is giving us a king genuinely wrestling with a hard providence. The next verses will show his journey from anger to fear to learning.

And David was afraid of the LORD that day, and said, How shall the ark of the LORD come to me? So David would not remove the ark of the LORD unto him into the city of David: but David carried it aside into the house of Obed-edom the Gittite. Guidance: The displeasure shifts to fear. 'David was afraid of the LORD that day, and said, How shall the ark of the LORD come to me?' David realizes he is dealing with something larger than he had calculated. The fear is the right emotion. The presence of God is not a domesticated artifact; he cannot transport it like a king's treasure. The question 'how shall the ark of the LORD come to me?' is the chapter's pivot. David is not abandoning the project; he is asking what he had failed to ask before — what is the right way? He diverts the ark to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite. 'Gittite' means 'man of Gath' — possibly a Philistine convert, or more likely a Levite from a town called Gath-rimmon (1 Chr 26:4-8 names Obed-edom as a Levite gatekeeper, which is the more probable identification). The ark stops at his house. The chapter is giving David a three-month pause to study Torah and learn the proper transport. 1 Chronicles 15:2 records what David figured out in this period: 'None ought to carry the ark of God but the Levites: for them hath the LORD chosen to carry the ark of God, and to minister unto him for ever.' The Chronicler tells us David explicitly identifies the chapter 6 disaster as the result of skipping Torah on transport. The interruption is the LORD's teaching.

And the ark of the LORD continued in the house of Obed-edom the Gittite three months: and the LORD blessed Obed-edom, and all his household. Guidance: Three months. The ark sits in a Levite gatekeeper's house, and the LORD blesses him 'and all his household.' The narrator does not tell us how — perhaps abundance of crops, new children, healing, financial flourishing; the Hebrew is broad. The blessing is observable enough that all of Israel notices. The contrast with Uzzah is total. Uzzah, who reached out to grab the ark, died. Obed-edom, who received the ark properly into his home, was blessed. The same ark; the same God; opposite outcomes by the difference in posture and reverence. The chapter is making sure we see both sides of God's presence: lethal when handled wrongly, life-giving when received rightly. Three months will become the time-marker for a season of waiting before resuming a discontinued ministry. The LORD's correction of David's procession does not cancel the destination; it pauses the journey until the king is ready to do it right.

And it was told king David, saying, The LORD hath blessed the house of Obed-edom, and all that pertaineth unto him, because of the ark of God. So David went and brought up the ark of God from the house of Obed-edom into the city of David with gladness. Guidance: Word reaches David. The Obed-edom blessing is the signal that the season of pause has accomplished its work. The LORD's presence is now perceived as life-giving, not lethal — David can approach again. He goes 'and brought up the ark of God… with gladness.' The Hebrew bessimchah is the same word used in the joy-festivals of Israel; David is now joyful, not anxious. The Chronicler's parallel (1 Chr 15) tells us the prep David did during the three months: appointing Levitical porters, distinguishing the Kohathite carriers, sanctifying the priests, and assembling the worship orchestra in proper order. The chapter's omission of these details is deliberate; the narrator emphasizes the emotional turn — fear becoming gladness — while the Chronicler emphasizes the procedural turn — disorder becoming Torah-shaped order. Both are true. Both are needed.

And it was so, that when they that bare the ark of the LORD had gone six paces, he sacrificed oxen and fatlings. And David danced before the LORD with all his might; and David was girded with a linen ephod. Guidance: 'They that bare the ark' — the Hebrew explicitly says the carriers (the Levites) bear the ark, not a cart. David has corrected the transport. After six paces (the very first interval of carrying), they pause and sacrifice — bullock and fatling, the standard major offerings. Six steps without disaster is itself the LORD's confirmation that the new method is accepted. (1 Chronicles 15:26 says 'God helped the Levites that bare the ark.') Then 'David danced before the LORD with all his might.' The dance is total physical worship. He is wearing 'a linen ephod' — a simple priestly garment, not the royal robes one would expect for a state ceremony. He is publicly dressing as a worshiper, not a sovereign. The phrase 'with all his might' (bekol-oz) is the same intensity language used of loving God 'with all thy strength' in Deuteronomy 6:5. David is performing the central commandment in physical form: he is loving the LORD with all his strength on a Jerusalem street. The image is one of the great worship pictures in scripture — a king disrobed of royal finery, dancing before God in front of his people. The chapter is teaching us what corrected worship looks like: obedient in form, joyful in heart, humble in posture, total in body. There is no part of David held back from God in this verse.

So David and all the house of Israel brought up the ark of the LORD with shouting, and with the sound of the trumpet. And as the ark of the LORD came into the city of David, Michal Saul's daughter looked through a window, and saw king David leaping and dancing before the LORD; and she despised him in her heart. Guidance: 'David and all the house of Israel' — the same phrase as v5, but now done correctly. The ark arrives at the city of David accompanied by shouting and ram's horn trumpets — the standard Hebrew acclamation for royalty and victory. The procession reaches the city. Then the verse pivots to a window. Michal — the wife David had reclaimed from Phaltiel in chapter 3 — is watching from a palace window. She sees David 'leaping and dancing before the LORD.' Her response: 'she despised him in her heart.' The Hebrew bissah is the strongest possible contempt verb. Why? The narrator does not say outright in this verse; he will let Michal speak in v20. The probable reasons are layered. (1) Royal dignity: she had grown up in Saul's court, where the king did not dance in public. To her, dancing in a priestly ephod is degrading. (2) Personal history: David had reclaimed her by political deal; the marriage is bitter; the man she sees rejoicing in the streets is not the man she remembered loving (1 Sam 18:20). (3) Spiritual misalignment: she does not share his theology of worship-with-all-one's-might. The chapter is going to play out the consequence of the inner contempt. The window is the metaphor: she sees David from inside a palace, looking out; he is in the street, in the dust, before God. The marriage will not survive this.

And they brought in the ark of the LORD, and set it in his place, in the midst of the tabernacle that David had pitched for it: and David offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before the LORD. And as soon as David had made an end of offering burnt offerings and peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of the LORD of hosts. And he dealt among all the people, even among the whole multitude of Israel, as well to the women as men, to every one a cake of bread, and a good piece of flesh, and a flagon of wine. So all the people departed every one to his house. Guidance: The ark is installed in 'the tabernacle that David had pitched for it' — a special tent David has prepared in Jerusalem (the original Mosaic tabernacle was still at Gibeon, 1 Chr 21:29; David has built a second tent specifically for the ark in Jerusalem until the temple will be built decades later). David offers burnt offerings (entirely consumed on the altar) and peace offerings (shared by worshipers in a communal meal). Then he blesses the people 'in the name of the LORD of hosts.' The king functions in a quasi-priestly role here — leading worship and pronouncing blessing — a feature of the Israelite monarchy David and Solomon will exercise that the prophetic tradition will later qualify. Then the distribution: 'a cake of bread, and a good piece of flesh, and a flagon of wine' to every Israelite present. The Hebrew eshpar (translated 'good piece of flesh') is uncertain — perhaps 'date cake,' perhaps 'measure of meat.' Regardless, the distribution is national: every man, every woman, gets the festival ration. This is the inaugural state feast of the unified kingdom. David is paying for the entire country's worship-meal out of the royal stores. The throne is using its wealth to feed the LORD's people on the LORD's special day. There is a category of generosity that defines a reign in its first major ceremony. David sets the precedent. 'So all the people departed every one to his house' — they go home full of bread, meat, wine, and worship. The day has been complete.

Then David returned to bless his household. And Michal the daughter of Saul came out to meet David, and said, How glorious was the king of Israel to day, who uncovered himself to day in the eyes of the handmaids of his servants, as one of the vain fellows shamelessly uncovereth himself! Guidance: David comes home to bless his own household — the natural extension of having just blessed the nation. Michal meets him at the door. The sarcasm of her opening line is dripping: 'How glorious was the king of Israel today, who uncovered himself today in the eyes of the handmaids of his servants, as one of the vain fellows shamelessly uncovereth himself!' Her accusation: dancing in a linen ephod meant his royal undergarments were visible to the slave-girls in the crowd; he stripped down to a worshiper's outfit in front of women who should not have seen the king half-clothed. The Hebrew rekim ('vain fellows') is contemptuous — empty, worthless, undignified men. She is calling him a clown in a worship procession. Notice the narrator's relentless designation: 'Michal the daughter of Saul.' Not 'Michal his wife.' She is being identified by her father, not her husband. The marriage is over in the narrator's mind; Michal speaks now as Saul's daughter — defending the dignity of the previous king's court against the messy religious enthusiasm of the new king. The clash is dynastic and theological at once. Michal has internalized the Saulide model of kingship: dignified, distant, reserved. David has internalized the David-after-God's-own-heart model: total worship, vulnerability, no royal dignity above God. The two models cannot share a household.

And David said unto Michal, It was before the LORD, which chose me before thy father, and before all his house, to appoint me ruler over the people of the LORD, over Israel: therefore will I play before the LORD. And I will yet be more vile than thus, and will be base in mine own sight: and of the maidservants which thou hast spoken of, of them shall I be had in honour. Guidance: David's response is theological, sharp, and unyielding. (1) 'It was before the LORD.' Whatever Michal saw was for an audience of One. (2) 'Which chose me before thy father, and before all his house.' He invokes the LORD's choice — Samuel's anointing at Bethlehem twenty years ago, the rejection of Saul, the line that runs through David. The reference is brutal: David reminds Michal that her father was the king the LORD rejected and David is the king the LORD chose. The chapter has just enacted that theology in public; her contempt is, at the spiritual level, contempt for the LORD's choice. (3) 'Therefore will I play before the LORD.' Direct response to her accusation. He affirms the dancing and commits to repeat it. (4) 'I will yet be more vile than thus, and will be base in mine own sight.' David doubles down on his self-abasement before God. He will be even more undignified, even more 'base' (humble in his own self-estimation), because public humbling before God is exactly what kingship under the LORD requires. (5) 'Of the maidservants… of them shall I be had in honour.' The very women Michal said witnessed his shame are the ones who will honor him. The slave-girls and ordinary people get David's theology; Michal does not. The chapter is showing us the cost of being chosen for the kingdom that the LORD's anointed must take. There is a category of worship that the political class will despise and the ordinary worshipers will love. David lives in that category and accepts the cost.

And David said unto Michal, It was before the LORD, which chose me before thy father, and before all his house, to appoint me ruler over the people of the LORD, over Israel: therefore will I play before the LORD. And I will yet be more vile than thus, and will be base in mine own sight: and of the maidservants which thou hast spoken of, of them shall I be had in honour. Guidance: David's response is theological, sharp, and unyielding. (1) 'It was before the LORD.' Whatever Michal saw was for an audience of One. (2) 'Which chose me before thy father, and before all his house.' He invokes the LORD's choice — Samuel's anointing at Bethlehem twenty years ago, the rejection of Saul, the line that runs through David. The reference is brutal: David reminds Michal that her father was the king the LORD rejected and David is the king the LORD chose. The chapter has just enacted that theology in public; her contempt is, at the spiritual level, contempt for the LORD's choice. (3) 'Therefore will I play before the LORD.' Direct response to her accusation. He affirms the dancing and commits to repeat it. (4) 'I will yet be more vile than thus, and will be base in mine own sight.' David doubles down on his self-abasement before God. He will be even more undignified, even more 'base' (humble in his own self-estimation), because public humbling before God is exactly what kingship under the LORD requires. (5) 'Of the maidservants… of them shall I be had in honour.' The very women Michal said witnessed his shame are the ones who will honor him. The slave-girls and ordinary people get David's theology; Michal does not. The chapter is showing us the cost of being chosen for the kingdom that the LORD's anointed must take. There is a category of worship that the political class will despise and the ordinary worshipers will love. David lives in that category and accepts the cost.

Therefore Michal the daughter of Saul had no child unto the day of her death. Guidance: One verse, one fact, the closing of a relationship. 'Michal the daughter of Saul' — the narrator's designation once more, for the last time — 'had no child unto the day of her death.' The Hebrew is unspecific about cause. Some readers see divine judgment on Michal for her contempt; others read it as David's withdrawing from the marriage after the confrontation (the marriage continues legally but is sexually over). Either way, the Saulide line through Michal is permanently terminated. The political marriage that was supposed to give David's house a Saulide heir produces no heir; the Davidic line will run through other wives. The two royal houses, which Phaltiel's tears in 3:16 had already mourned, are now formally separated. The chapter ends with a marriage in ruins and a kingdom in worship. The two outcomes are not unrelated; the husband who chose worship over royal dignity discovers that the wife who chose royal dignity over worship has nothing further to share with him. Tomorrow, chapter 7, David will receive the LORD's covenant promise that gives the Davidic dynasty its theological foundation — and the foundation passes through a different wife (Bathsheba, eventually). Michal's contempt has not stopped the LORD's covenant; it has only ended Michal's part in it. The chapter is a sober reminder that worship-postures-of-the-heart shape the future of households as much as battles shape the future of nations. The king who danced before the ark will have an heir; the wife who despised him from the window will have none. The throne carries on into chapter 7. The household scar of chapter 6 will run forward through the rest of David's reign.
